transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Grace. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately. There’s just a lot going on with my parents and the divorce. It’s hard to know how to navigate it all. I feel like they don’t really get what I’m going through, and that makes things even more complicated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy load with everything happening around your parents’ divorce. Feeling overwhelmed is completely understandable, especially when it seems like they might not fully understand your experience. Can you share a bit more about what specifically feels overwhelming for you right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... every time I think about visiting them, I get this knot in my stomach. I worry about what they’ll say or how they’ll react. It’s like I’m anticipating criticism, and that makes me hesitant to even reach out. I want to talk to them, but I also feel like they might not be supportive. It’s just this constant back and forth in my head.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That knot in your stomach sounds really uncomfortable, and it makes sense that you’d feel hesitant to reach out given your worries about their reactions. It seems like there’s a conflict between your desire to connect with them and the fear of not being supported. Can you tell me more about what you anticipate they might say or how they might react that contributes to those feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I just worry that they’ll be critical or dismissive. Like, they might not see how hard this is for me. Sometimes it feels like they’re more focused on their own feelings about the divorce rather than understanding mine. I keep thinking, “If they can’t support me, who will?” It’s just... frustrating. I want to feel like I can talk to them, but I also don’t want to feel judged or misunderstood.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really frustrating to feel like your parents might be more focused on their own experiences rather than being there for you. That thought of “If they can’t support me, who will?” seems to weigh heavily on you. It’s understandable to want to feel heard and understood, especially during such a challenging time. How do you usually cope with those feelings of frustration and uncertainty when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I tend to just withdraw a bit. I don’t really reach out to them or talk about what I’m feeling. It’s easier to keep things to myself, even though it feels lonely. Sometimes I try to distract myself with other things, like reading or watching shows, but it doesn’t really solve anything. I guess I just hope that maybe things will get better on their own, even though I know that’s not really a solution.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Withdrawing and keeping things to yourself can feel like a protective measure, even if it leads to loneliness. It sounds like you’re aware that distracting yourself doesn’t truly address what you’re feeling, yet it’s a way to cope with the discomfort. It’s understandable to hope for things to improve on their own, especially when it feels overwhelming. What do you think holds you back from reaching out to them, even when you know you want to connect?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s just the fear of how they might react. I worry that if I open up, they might not respond the way I need them to. What if they don’t understand or they just make me feel worse? It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of wanting to talk but being scared of the outcome. I guess I just don’t want to feel more hurt or rejected than I already do.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That fear of how they might react really seems to keep you in a tough spot. It’s understandable to want to protect yourself from feeling more hurt or rejected, especially when you’re already dealing with so much. It sounds like there’s a lot of vulnerability involved in considering opening up to them. How do you feel about that loop of wanting to talk but being scared of the outcome? What does that experience feel like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It feels really exhausting, honestly. It’s like I’m constantly weighing my options, and no matter what I choose, I end up feeling stuck. I want to be brave and reach out, but then I get hit with all these “what ifs” that make me retreat. It’s frustrating because I know I need support, but I also feel like I’m just going in circles. It’s hard to find a way out of that cycle, and it leaves me feeling even more alone.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That exhaustion from feeling stuck in that cycle is completely understandable. It sounds like you’re caught between wanting to be brave and the weight of those “what ifs” that pull you back. It must be really frustrating to feel like you need support but find yourself feeling more isolated because of it. When you think about breaking that cycle, what comes to mind for you? What would it look like to take a step forward, even if it feels small?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think... maybe it would help to start with something small, like just sending a text or an email instead of having a face-to-face conversation right away. That way, I could express how I’m feeling without the immediate pressure of a conversation. But even thinking about that makes me anxious. What if they don’t respond well? It feels like a risk, but I guess it could also be a way to test the waters. I just wish it didn’t feel so daunting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting with a text or email sounds like a thoughtful approach to ease into the conversation. It makes sense that it feels daunting, especially with the fear of how they might respond. Taking that small step can feel like a risk, but it also seems like it could provide you with some clarity about their reactions. It’s okay to feel anxious about it; that’s a natural response. What do you think might help you manage that anxiety as you consider taking this step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe writing down what I want to say could help. It might make it feel less overwhelming if I have my thoughts organized. I could also remind myself that it’s okay if they don’t respond the way I hope. Just reaching out could be a step forward, even if it doesn’t go perfectly. I guess I just need to find a way to be kind to myself in the process, you know? It’s just hard to remember that sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Writing down your thoughts sounds like a great way to organize what you want to say and make the process feel more manageable. It’s really insightful that you recognize the importance of being kind to yourself, especially when navigating such a challenging situation. It can be easy to forget that self-compassion when you’re feeling anxious. How do you think you might remind yourself to be kind to yourself during this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could set some reminders for myself, like little notes or affirmations to read when I start feeling anxious. Something simple, like “It’s okay to feel this way” or “You’re doing your best.” I could also try to take a few deep breaths before I reach out, just to calm myself down a bit. I guess it’s about finding those small ways to be gentle with myself, even when things feel tough. It’s just... sometimes I forget to do that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those reminders and affirmations sound like a wonderful way to cultivate self-compassion, especially during tough moments. Taking deep breaths can also be a helpful tool to ground yourself before reaching out. It’s completely normal to forget to be gentle with yourself, especially when you’re feeling overwhelmed. It seems like you’re starting to build a toolkit for navigating these feelings, which is a positive step. What do you think might help you remember to use those tools when you need them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could keep the notes somewhere visible, like on my fridge or in my planner, so I see them often. I could also set a specific time to check in with myself, like at the end of the day, to reflect on how I’m feeling and remind myself to use those tools. It might help to have a routine around it, so it becomes a habit. I just hope I can stick to it when things get tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Keeping the notes visible and setting a specific time to check in with yourself sounds like a solid plan. Creating a routine can definitely help make those practices feel more natural over time. It’s understandable to worry about sticking to it, especially when things get tough. What do you think might motivate you to keep that routine going, even when you’re feeling overwhelmed?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reminding myself of how much I want to feel better could help. If I can focus on the idea that these small steps might lead to a little more peace or clarity, it might motivate me to stick with it. Also, maybe thinking about how reaching out could improve my relationships, even if it’s just a little, could keep me going. I want to feel more connected and supported, and I guess that’s worth the effort, even if it feels hard right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a powerful motivation—to focus on the potential for more peace and clarity, as well as improved connections with your relationships. It sounds like you have a strong desire for support and connection, which is completely valid. Keeping that in mind can help you push through the discomfort of taking those small steps. As you think about this journey ahead, what do you feel is the most important thing you want to hold onto as you move forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the most important thing I want to hold onto is the idea that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that reaching out doesn’t have to be perfect. I want to remind myself that it’s a process, and it’s okay to take it one step at a time. I also want to remember that I deserve support and understanding, even if it feels scary to ask for it. Just holding onto that thought might help me be more open to taking those steps, even when it feels tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful insight, Grace. Embracing vulnerability and recognizing that it’s okay for the process to be imperfect can really empower you as you take those steps. It’s important to remind yourself that you deserve support and understanding, and it sounds like you’re cultivating a compassionate mindset toward yourself. As you move forward, how do you think you might celebrate or acknowledge your progress, no matter how small?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could celebrate my progress by acknowledging the small steps I take, even if they seem minor. Maybe I could treat myself to something I enjoy, like a nice cup of coffee or a little time to relax with a book. I could also keep a journal to write down what I’ve accomplished, no matter how small, so I can look back and see how far I’ve come. Just recognizing those moments might help me feel more encouraged to keep going.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like lovely ways to celebrate your progress! Treating yourself to something you enjoy and keeping a journal to reflect on your accomplishments can really reinforce your efforts and help you see the growth over time. It’s wonderful that you’re thinking about how to acknowledge even the small steps, as they can add up to significant change. As you continue on this journey, what do you think will be the first small step you’d like to take?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the first small step I’d like to take is writing down what I want to say in that text or email to my parents. Just getting my thoughts organized might help me feel more prepared and less anxious about reaching out. Once I have that down, I can decide when to send it. It feels like a manageable way to start, and I think it could help me feel a bit more in control of the situation.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great first step, Grace! Organizing your thoughts can really help you feel more prepared and in control. Taking that manageable action can also ease some of the anxiety around reaching out. It’s wonderful that you’re setting yourself up for success in this way. As you work on that, how do you feel about checking in with yourself afterward to reflect on the experience?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself afterward could be really helpful. It would give me a chance to acknowledge how I felt during the process and what I might have learned from it. I could take a moment to recognize any feelings that come up, whether it’s relief, anxiety, or something else. It might help me process the experience and remind me that I’m taking steps, even if they’re small. I guess it’s about being gentle with myself no matter what happens.
